title: National Policy and the Panoceanic Navy
description: The case for transitioning from a power projection navy to one focused on sea denial and control.
text:
In his 1954 Proceedings article “National Policy and the Transoceanic Navy,” Samuel Huntington defined the elements of a military service. He argued that a service must first determine its strategic concept: how it will implement national policy and defend the nation against a threat. Then, if the public values the service’s intended contributions to national policy, the service can earn the resources necessary to implement its strategic concept. Finally, a service’s organizational structure allows it to group those resources to best implement its strategic concept.1
Huntington applied that framework to the U.S. Navy’s history. In the Continental Phase, from the nation’s founding until the 1890s, the United States fought for North American dominance. Because most threats were on land, the Navy played a subordinate role to the Army and focused primarily on coastal defense, commerce protection and raiding, and support of forces ashore, earning weak public support and limited resources as a result. The Oceanic Phase, from the 1890s until the end of World War II, focused on national efforts to achieve supremacy against threats emanating from the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. Based on that national aim and shaped by the writings of Alfred Thayer Mahan, the Navy sought to achieve command of the sea through the construction of a powerful battle fleet, giving it a convincing strategic concept that earned the service great public support and resources. Finally, Huntington introduced the Eurasian phase of national policy that started at the end of World War II and featured the replacement of oceanic threats with a new continental threat, the Soviet Union. He then introduced the transoceanic Navy, which would use command of the sea to influence events ashore and give it a new strategic concept to earn public support and resources.2
Part I: The Mission
Today, the nation’s primary threat has shifted from the Soviet Union to China, which has an economy, military, and set of ambitions that make it a fundamentally different and more menacing adversary. As a result, Huntington’s Eurasian Phase is over and a new phase of national policy has begun, to be dominated by diplomatic, informational, military, and economic competition between the United States and China. Within that competition, China’s construction of a fleet and military designed to challenge the U.S. Navy has degraded U.S. command of the sea to its lowest point in 80 years, undermining the transoceanic Navy’s strategic concept and ability to contribute to national policy.3
In its place a new doctrine is taking shape, the theory of the panoceanic Navy, shifting away from projecting power ashore and toward reestablishing command of the sea to enable the flow of friendly military forces and trade while denying that movement to the adversary.4 That strategic focus would allow the U.S. Navy to best protect the homeland, lead the joint force in implementing a strategy of denial against China, protect the nation’s trade and thus its wealth and power, and ultimately preserve the American way of life.5
Using the same article structure as Huntington, this work explores the basic elements of that new doctrine—the service’s emerging strategic concept, that concept’s impact on the Navy’s relationship with and ability to earn resources from the American people, and the organizational structure that best groups and uses those resources.
The Crisis of the Navy
Sea control is an operational function focused on using a portion of the sea for one’s own purpose for a limited time, such as to allow an amphibious ship to land Marines or a merchant ship to sail safely through a strait to conduct trade.6 In contrast, command of the sea is the long-term strategic condition that deals with “the balance of perceived strength among contending navies,” in which one navy is so powerful that others decline to challenge it.7 As Alfred Thayer Mahan described it:
It is not the taking of individual ships or convoys, be they few or many, that strikes down the money power of a nation; it is possession of that overbearing power on the sea which drives the enemy’s flag from it, or allows it to appear only as a fugitive, and which, by controlling the great common, closes the highways by which commerce moves to and from the enemy’s shores. This overbearing power can only be exercised by great navies.”8
The U.S. Navy established command of the sea following World War II. That victory, Huntington showed, created a paradoxical crisis: The Navy had built a fleet to establish command of the sea and then achieved just that. Its success meant there were no credible adversaries at sea, undermining the service’s long-held strategic concept that it would guard against oceanic threats and needed a large fleet to do so. The Navy’s ability to win public support and earn the resources necessary to contribute to national objectives suffered as a result.
Huntington then introduced the transoceanic Navy, a service whose “purpose now is not to acquire command of the sea but rather to utilize its command of the sea to achieve supremacy on the land.”9 For 80 years the Navy skillfully used that command, moving its fleet where and when required to enable trade and project striking and amphibious power into the Eurasian continent, such as in Korea, Vietnam, Iran, Iraq, Kosovo, and Afghanistan, while threatening to do so against the Soviet Union, as epitomized in the 1986 Maritime Strategy. Other than a brief challenge in the 1970s and ’80s from a powerful Soviet submarine force, U.S. command of the sea has not been in doubt since World War II. The Navy has repeatedly used it to the nation’s great benefit.10
Today the Navy has numerous problems it is addressing, including shipbuilding capacity, maintenance delays, and personnel retention. However, it has just one crisis now affecting every aspect of the service and its ability to contribute to national objectives: It no longer enjoys the uncontested command of the sea that has underpinned its strategic concept since 1945.
The primary challenger to that command is the People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLAN), now the world’s largest navy by hull count—if not yet by tonnage—with approximately 25 percent more warships than the U.S. Navy, a disparity expected to grow to almost 50 percent by 2030.11 Approximately 70 percent of those PLAN warships have been launched since 2010, compared to just 25 percent of U.S. warships, meaning it is no longer a fleet of old, obsolete coastal vessels.12
Supporting the PLAN, the PLA Air Force (PLAAF) is a “comprehensive strategic air force capable of long-range airpower projection,” while the PLA Rocket Force (PLARF) has the “the capability to conduct long-range precision strikes against ships, including aircraft carriers, out to the Western Pacific from mainland China,” according to the Department of Defense (DoD).13 The PLA has multiple types of operationally fielded hypersonic and ballistic antiship missiles; the U.S. Navy has none.14
The Navy’s strenuous submarine production efforts and the Marine Corps’ Force Design are implicit acknowledgements that the United States no longer enjoys uncontested command of the sea. Large numbers of fast-attack submarines are only necessary if there are large numbers of warships for them to sink and large areas in which surface ships would be at too great a risk to operate. Similarly, Marine littoral regiments are only necessary if Marines must “stand in” a high-threat zone, at risk of being cut off from naval support; medium landing ships (LSMs) are only desirable if large amphibious warships cannot deliver Marines where and when desired; and the Marine Corps’ sea control efforts are only necessary if that control is contested.15
However, losing command of the seas does not mean the U.S. Navy has lost the seas altogether or that the PLAN now enjoys that command. The PLAN has its own challenges, including lack of combat experience, periodic bouts of corruption in its senior ranks, a rigid command structure, and dependence on foreign sources of energy.16 The point is not whether the U.S. Navy or PLAN would prevail in a fight for the seas, it is that there could be such a fight at all; that someone has built a fleet to challenge the U.S. Navy and that U.S. naval supremacy is no longer a given.
The PLAN is by far the Navy’s most serious competitor, but it is not the only one striving to degrade U.S. command of the sea. Russia is developing advanced weapons and operates capable submarines such as the Severodvinsk-class guided-missile submarines (SSGNs), which are “on par” with U.S. submarines and will pose a “persistent proximate threat.”17
Even nonstate actors, because of the proliferation and democratization of long-range sensors, networks, and weapons, are able to contest that command, at least on a regional basis. The Houthis launched more attacks on U.S. Navy ships in two years than all other adversaries had in the preceding 79 years.18
In 1945, the Navy had built a fleet to fight for sea control but then won command of the sea and found itself confronted with a continental threat, and so it needed a new strategic concept. Today, the Navy is built to project power ashore against continental threats but no longer enjoys the command of the sea necessary to do so. Instead, it faces a new maritime power, meaning it again needs a new strategic concept. That concept is now emerging, already described and shaped by naval leaders and writers.19 As with the start of the transoceanic Navy, however, this new doctrine, as Huntington wrote, “will require a fundamental revolution in naval thinking.” To describe that doctrine first requires examining the national policy it will support.
National Policy in the Pacific Phase
The U.S. military seeks to protect the nation and its people from foreign attack, and all three national defense strategies (NDSs) since 2017 have prioritized homeland defense.20 While that ranking has not changed, the threats to the homeland and the strategies used to counter them have. Today, China, Russia, North Korea, Iran, and nonstate actors all have varying abilities to strike the United States with conventional or nuclear weapons, and so the U.S. military must be prepared to defend against them all. However, China stands alone as a threat because it also has the intent and capability to reshape the international order in its favor and thus diminish U.S. military and economic power.21
China now has the world’s second largest economy, manufactures more goods than the next four leading countries combined, and is a global leader in research, innovation, and patents.22 It has the world’s largest navy, army, conventional rocket force, merchant marine, maritime law enforcement fleet, and the Indo-Pacific’s largest aviation force.23
China’s rise as the United States’ dominant threat initiated a new phase in national policy, as Huntington’s Continental, Oceanic, and Eurasian phases give way to what can be called the “Pacific Phase.” Strategist Hal Brands described the competition defining that phase:
Of all today’s crises and conflicts, the U.S.-China rivalry will most fundamentally remake our world. A contest between the two top powers will shape the international system and the lives of people everywhere. At best, it will be a long, tense struggle—a “New Cold War”—lasting many years. At worst, it could explode into nuclear catastrophe. For Americans, winning this competition will be the central challenge of our time.24
In response, the United States has devoted increasing levels of attention and resources to address that threat—the 2011 Pivot to Asia, the 2018 NDS’s declaration that China was the nation’s primary strategic competitor, the 2021 formation of the AUKUS trilateral security partnership, and increasingly tough diplomatic and economic measures spanning the administrations of both parties.25 Those efforts are culminating in a strategy of denial intended to deter—and if necessary, defeat—Chinese expansionist ambitions. That strategy aims to prevent China from seizing additional territory, becoming the hegemon of Asia, and thus posing an even greater threat to U.S. trade, wealth, and power—and ultimately the U.S. homeland.26
The challenge for the Navy is to determine what strategic concept will best align it with those national aims. How can the Navy best defend the homeland and counter the nation’s primary threat, and thus earn the public support and resources necessary to implement that concept?
A New Naval Doctrine: The Panoceanic Navy
Whereas the transoceanic Navy shifted focus “away from the oceans and towards the land masses on their far side,” the focus now returns to the sea. The proliferation of long-range sensors, networks, and weapons, however, means it is increasingly difficult for any fleet to achieve sea control—never mind command of the sea. In addition, while conflicts in the Oceanic Phase were primarily decided by fleet-on-fleet actions, the proliferation and democratization of technologies means non-naval forces (Houthis with land-based antiship missiles on the low end of the spectrum and the PLARF on the high end, for example) now have an unprecedented ability to engage fleets, greatly expanding the range and scope of the naval battlefield.27 What results is the theory of the panoceanic Navy.
The Distribution of International Power
The Eurasian Phase featured competition between the United States and its allies against the Soviet Union and its client states, and later against post–Cold War regional threats. In the Pacific Phase, power is primarily distributed between the United States and its allies against China. There are other threats to U.S. security, but they are pale in comparison. Per the DoD: “[China] is the only competitor to the United States with the intent and, increasingly, the capacity to reshape the international order.”28
The Site of Decisive Action
Huntington argued that the Oceanic Phase featured competition between naval powers, and so the site of decisive action was at sea. Following World War II, the Eurasian Phase featured a naval power against a continental power and so “the locale of decisive actions has switched from the sea to the land: not the inner heart of the land mass, but rather to the coastal area . . . the Littoral.”29
The Pacific phase now features competition between two nations with powerful fleets and large maritime trade flows, and so the site of decisive action has shifted back to the sea. Whoever prevails there will win the ability to influence events ashore, whether through military functions, such as power projection and deterrence, or through the protection and denial of trade and accumulation of national wealth and power. Even the simple viewing of a map makes it obvious: The two competing superpowers are separated by the world’s largest ocean, and it is here that the military competition will be decided.
The Mission of the Navy
Today, the threat, the nation’s strategy, and the Navy’s ability to command the sea have all changed, and so too must the Navy’s strategic concept. The Navy’s task is now the same as it has been for all navies faced with credible adversaries: acquire command of the sea to enable the flow of friendly military forces and trade while denying that movement to the enemy.30 To accomplish that strategic aim requires the Navy to prioritize sea control as its primary operational function or mission.
The nation’s adversaries typically must cross the oceans to attack the United States directly, making a navy focused on sea control well suited to defend against them. Russian ground forces pose little threat to U.S. soil; instead, Russia’s primary conventional threat to the United States is naval, such as land-attack missiles launched from the Severodvinsk-class SSGNs, which U.S. antisubmarine warfare forces can best defend against.31 North Korea, Iran, and nonstate actors, while not posing an existential threat, could still launch damaging attacks; the best U.S. defense against many conventional forces is a strong fleet operating forward that prevents them from ever reaching U.S. shores. Many nations, most prominently China and Russia and their ballistic-missile submarines and ground-based nuclear missiles, pose a nuclear threat, but strategic deterrence has proven effective and appears likely to continue to be—and those efforts are led by the Navy’s strategic deterrent submarines. Prioritizing sea control would enable the U.S. Navy to defeat threats from the Pacific, Atlantic, and Arctic Oceans, preserving those vast expanses as massive moats to defend the homeland.
A focus on sea control also would directly support a strategy of denial against China, as the Navy is best poised of all the services to move its forces across thousands of miles of the Pacific Ocean and deliver firepower against the PLA. That would also enable the other services to support a strategy of denial. In anything other than an episodic way, the joint force is not going to reach or sustain a fight against China—never mind win it—without sea control. Similarly, allies such as Japan, Australia, South Korea, and the Philippines are unlikely to be able to contribute to a U.S. strategy of denial without logistics support, which again requires the Navy to keep sea lines of communication open. Concentrating on sea control also offers the promise of restricting or blockading Chinese trade, which Mahan showed was the best means to “embarrass the finances of a country,” disrupt its economy and society, and create political leverage to end the war on U.S. terms.32
A focus on sea control, in support of the long-term goal of reestablishing command of the sea, offers the nation more than just wartime benefits. A strong Navy underpins U.S. trade, ensuring goods reach U.S. households and businesses can ship U.S.-made food, energy, commodities, and products throughout the global marketplace without threat of attacks, seizures, or refusals. Mahan showed that overseas trade is the primary driver of national wealth, which in turn gives the United States the military, economic, diplomatic, and humanitarian power it has enjoyed since World War II. Because the vast majority of trade moves by sea, the Navy has a peacetime responsibility to keep the sea lines open. Weak forces such as the Houthis have threatened those lines on a regional basis; strong powers such as China, if not deterred, could close the global commons and fundamentally disrupt the U.S. economy, degrade the nation’s power, and threaten the American way of life.33
The Navy has enduring functions and global responsibilities other than high-end sea control. However, its primary doctrinal guidance makes clear that sea control is the “one constant” that “enables all other naval functions.”34 The Navy’s long-term ability to project power, deter adversaries, and meet its global responsibilities will diminish if it cannot complete its primary mission against its primary adversary in the primary theater.
While reestablishing command of the sea is the Navy’s goal, tactics must inform that strategic aim. As the late Captain Wayne Hughes wrote, “Strategy must rest on the rock of combat capability.”35 And the problem with a focus on sea control to reestablish command of the sea is that the proliferation of sensor, network, and weapon technologies has made it, tactically, an incredibly difficult mission. At sea, the PLAN has equipped most of its warships, aircraft, submarines, and larger robotic and autonomous systems (RASs) with antiship missiles, meaning almost every platform in its fleet, not just capital ships, poses a significant threat. Even if Chinese aircraft carriers, cruisers, and destroyers could be defeated, clandestine forces such as the world’s largest submarine fleet, PLA mining—forces, and the Chinese maritime militia—could significantly delay if not prevent U.S. sea control.36
Worsening the problem is the threat posed by forces in other domains. The transoceanic Navy was focused on using a sanctuary in the naval domain to project power into the land domain. Now the reverse is happening, as other domains may be at least partial sanctuaries from which forces can influence the naval domain. In the land domain, the PLARF operates from mobile launchers that are difficult to find and uses weapons that outrange most of those in the U.S. fleet, giving it the ability to strike ships thousands of miles from the coast.37 The U.S. Marine Corps’ Force Design is focused on using distributed and hidden stand-in forces to affect events at sea, a model the Houthis used to contest U.S. sea control efforts for almost two years. A ship has always been a fool to fight a fort, and now the fort (land-based forces) is mobile, has excellent scouting, long-range communications and weapons, and may be harder to find than the ship.
In the aerial domain, whereas the transoceanic Navy experienced small numbers of isolated attacks on its ships during wars in Korea, Vietnam, and the Middle East, today’s Navy faces a PLAAF whose primary mission is antisurface warfare, which it can carry out to the second island chain from bases in mainland China.38 The space and cyber domains have fundamentally changed naval warfare and are both poised to have great effect on events at sea. With tools such as multisensor satellites and Starlink communications constellations, surveillance and communication networks have never been cheaper to build or harder to disable, enabling attacks at very long ranges.39 Naval history has been dominated by desperate struggles to find the enemy, but today the space and cyber domains may reduce the time necessary to do so from months to minutes. Overall, as Captain Hughes wrote, “A naval force-on-force action need no longer be ship on ship,” and technological developments have “required a reinterpretation of ‘naval’ forces.”40
Given this reality, the panoceanic Navy will be forced to fight across great distances and multiple domains, where an imagery analyst in Nanjing, a cyber specialist at Fort Meade, a technician on a transporter-erector-launcher in Qingyang, a pilot based in Missouri, a RAS operator in Ningbo, and a Marine in Okinawa may have just as much effect on a naval engagement as a warship captain in the Philippine Sea. Whereas the oceanic Navy focused on fleet-on-fleet actions in a constrained part of an ocean and the transoceanic Navy focused on engagements between the fleet and continental forces in constrained littoral regions, the panoceanic Navy will have to strive to establish sea control against a wide array of military forces operating from multiple domains, with sensors, networks, and weapons of such long range to make it seem like the oceans are one continuous battlefield—a panoceanic arena.
Taken together, these trends have lowered the obstacles to a credible attack and raised the requirements for credible defense, making sea denial easier and sea control harder. That has already played out in regional contests. In the Red Sea, to attempt sea denial, the Houthis needed a truck, a computer, and a RAS, whereas the U.S. Navy, to achieve sea control, needed a guided-missile destroyer with a highly trained crew, phased-array radars and satellite networks, and multiple types of advanced interceptors. The Houthis might not have known themselves whether their attacks failed; the world would have known almost immediately had U.S. defenses failed. The Houthis capitalized on that disparity to contest U.S. Navy sea control efforts for almost two years without a navy, without an air force, and without a credible industrial base. The Houthis’ sea-denial efforts must be assessed as partially successful—despite U.S. naval officers’ and sailors’ superb performance in battle—as evidenced by the significant reductions in merchant shipping willing to risk a Red Sea transit.41 Similarly, the Ukrainians denied the Black Sea to the Russians primarily by using land-based missiles and RASs, and in doing so established a measure of sea control for their own maritime shipping.
Thus, while sea control is the Navy’s operational aim, in support of a long-term strategic goal of reestablishing U.S. command of the sea, technological trends and the democratization of naval power mean there are large portions of the ocean in which even the most capable fleets will, at best, be able to achieve sea denial.42 Given the threats the United States now faces, the nation’s resulting strategy, and the reality of modern combat at sea, the Navy’s mission is now to establish sea control where possible and sea denial where required. In peace and war, the Navy must protect the sea lanes against threats emanating from multiple domains and spanning the full range of capability, from the Houthis to the Russians to the Chinese. When that is not possible, the Navy must be able to create a nautical no-man’s land and deny enemies the use of the seas in support of their goals, whether they are to threaten the U.S. homeland or to establish regional hegemonies. In particular, the Navy must be able to deny China’s most important ambition, the seizure of Taiwan. U.S. trade, wealth, and power, the denial of Chinese hegemony in Asia—and ultimately the safety of the U.S. homeland—therefore all depend on getting naval strategy and force structure right.
For the transoceanic Navy, Huntington identified the basic weapons available in 1954 the Navy would use to conduct its new mission: carrier-based air power, fleet-based amphibious power, and naval artillery. For the panoceanic Navy to conduct its new mission, the primary weapons will be:
• Missiles distributed onto ships, submarines, aircraft, RASs, and ground-based launchers
• Torpedoes fired from submarines
• One-way attack RASs launched from a variety of ships, submarines, aircraft, or other RASs and land-based forces
As Huntington wrote, “The navy of the future will have to be organized around these basic weapons,” a shift that will again “require an entirely different sort of Navy.”
Part II: The Fleet
In “National Policy and the Transoceanic Navy,” Huntington defined the elements of a military service: a strategic concept to contribute to national policy, the public support and resources necessary to implement that policy, and the organization to best group those resources.
Given the shift in primary threat from the Soviet Union to China, the nation’s resulting change in strategy, and the degradation of U.S. command of the sea, the U.S. Navy needs a new strategic concept. Instead of the transoceanic Navy, the “panoceanic Navy” features a service shifting away from projecting power ashore to reestablishing Mahanian command of the sea to enable the flow of friendly military forces and trade while denying that movement to the enemy. This fundamental change in mission requires rethinking the fleet architecture that can best execute the Navy’s new mission, including the other two elements critical for a military service: public support and organization.
The Base of the Navy
In the Navy’s Oceanic Phase, from the 1890s to 1945, the seas were the site of decisive action and so the Navy’s base was ashore. For the transoceanic Navy, the site of decisive action was ashore and the seas were secure, so the Navy created a “floating base” system to move the base closer to the scene of operations. Huntington identified those “floating bases” as aircraft carriers supporting naval air power, amphibious warships supporting ground forces, and large surface warships supporting naval artillery and missiles.43 U.S. command of the sea allowed the Navy to move those bases close to the enemy’s shores, such as during the Vietnam War when the fleet projected power ashore from Yankee Station less than 100 miles from the coast.44
For the panoceanic Navy, the seas are again the site of decisive action, meaning those floating bases will be challenged. Adversaries are not fools; why attempt to individually engage dozens of high-performance aircraft, thousands of Marines, or hundreds of missiles if one could simultaneously defeat them all in an attack on their floating bases? The proliferation of sensors, networks, and weapons gives adversaries that opportunity.
In response, the Navy can improve its floating bases’ survivability and security through the same three means militaries have always considered: avoid being targeted (with antiscouting efforts such as cover, stealth, deception, and evasion, as submarines strive to do), avoid being hit (by increasing range to the threat and using defensive systems, as bomber air bases strive to do), and avoid being destroyed (by improving resilience with armor and damage control, as battleships strove to do). The problem is the viability of each of those options is dwindling.
Regarding the first option (avoid being targeted), the Navy can and should strive to achieve that aim, but the proliferation of sensors and networks, especially in space, makes it incredibly difficult to hide any platform today. Floating bases worsen that problem because they imply concentration, and concentration means large signatures that are even more difficult to hide. Attempting to conceal the largest warships from the most capable adversary the Navy has ever faced in the age of ubiquitous sensing appears inadvisable.45 The undersea domain remains a partial exception to this broader trend, based on the physical properties of water and the reality that submarines are getting quieter and the oceans are getting louder.46 As a result, the undersea domain likely remains uniquely suited for supporting U.S. naval power and the panoceanic Navy’s goal of establishing sea denial.
Turning to the second option (avoid being hit by opening range and using defenses), the Navy can withdraw those floating bases to increase the distance from the threat, but long-range missiles distributed among hundreds of small platforms make it increasingly difficult to retreat far enough to prevent those ships from being targeted while still being able to contribute to the fight. Alternatively, defensive systems—no matter how well designed, resourced, and operated—are unlikely to perform to the standard needed for large floating bases simply because of attackers’ inherent advantages in numbers, physics, and time. The attacker succeeds by achieving anything better than a zero percent hit rate on a large target moving in two dimensions, whereas the defender must achieve no less than a 100 percent interception rate against small, fast inbound targets moving in three dimensions to avoid disaster.47 As Rear Admiral Ted Carter—then the President of the Naval War College—wrote more than a decade ago, “When envisioning a future war, one must now assume that advanced missiles can reliably penetrate active defenses and destroy or neutralize their objectives.”48
The third and final option (avoid being destroyed using armor and damage control) does not appear feasible in an era of supersonic weapons with specialized warheads. Most warships hit by missiles in recent decades—INS Eilat, HMS Sheffield, the USS Stark (FFG-31), and the Russian Navy’s Moskva—were mission kills or sunk after just one or two hits. Admiral Carter again: “No matter how heavily armored they are, ships cannot expect to retain their combat effectiveness after hits from specialized antiship munitions, given the precision and lethality of [long-range precision strike] systems.”49
As a result of those trends, the best option for the panoceanic Navy is distribution. That allows for platforms of smaller size and signature and with lower individual costs, leading to a larger fleet that is collectively harder to target and that retains a greater proportion of its combat effectiveness when it inevitably suffers individual platform losses.50 The Navy already has started this shift with the distributed maritime operations (DMO) concept, distributed amphibious power in the form of LSMs, and distributed surface ship power in the form of missile-armed medium unmanned surface vessels (MUSVs).52
However, with the site of decisive action now at sea and with any fleet’s ability to concentrate large forces at sea diminishing, the Navy’s true base will be ashore again, as it was during the Oceanic Phase. While that maximizes range from and defenses against the threat, even distant bases ashore will not be secure. Russian submarines and bombers were supposedly safe at their respective bases, yet Ukraine capitalized on the proliferation of sensors, networks, and weapons to attack them at their homeports and airfields, striking deeper into Russia than Napoleon or Hitler ever could.52 Thus, just as at sea, distribution ashore will also be necessary. Again, the Navy and Marine Corps have started this with expeditionary advanced base operations (EABO), and smaller platforms such as LSMs and MUSVs are attractive because they can be built, based, and maintained in numerous dispersed shipyards, ports, and warehouses not feasible for large, manned warships.53
The Geographic Focus of Naval Operations
For the transoceanic Navy, given the primary threat and site of decisive action, the geographic focus was on the Eurasian littorals and the Mediterranean Sea. For the panoceanic Navy, the sea is back to being the site of decisive action, and so the Navy’s main geographic focus must be in the Pacific. There will be other areas of competition, but this region has the world’s most important trade routes and is where China’s core ambitions and U.S. interests intersect. Over the long term, neither the U.S. Navy nor the PLAN can hope to exert global influence if it cannot conduct its primary mission against its primary adversary in the primary theater.
The Aim of Naval Tactics
To translate the panoceanic Navy’s new strategic focus and operational mission into tactical goals requires an examination of the maritime operating environment and trends in the “kill chain” at sea. That kill chain includes the basic steps of detecting and tracking the enemy, communicating that data and attack orders, launching an attack, overcoming defenses, and hitting the target, followed by battle damage assessment, all while sustaining the platforms and people conducting each of those steps.
Over the long term, the proliferation of sensors means it is easier to find platforms at sea. Long-range communications and networks mean it is easier to convey targeting data and orders over vast distances quickly. Advances in weapons’ propulsion, warheads, and guidance systems mean more platforms can launch more highly capable weapons at increasingly greater ranges. Those weapons’ advantages in numbers, physics, and speed mean defenses are increasingly challenged. And those weapons’ specialized warheads and higher speeds mean they have great destructive power when they hit their targets.54
In simplest terms, if a platform can be found, it probably can be shot at. If it can be shot at, offensive weapons’ significant advantages over defenses means the platform probably can be hit. If it can be hit, modern weapons’ destructive power means the platform probably can be seriously damaged, disabled, or destroyed. Overall, technological trends are making each step in the kill chain easier for the attacker and more challenging for the defender, a reality confronting both high-end platforms and the logistics forces that support them.
Based on that, Captain Hughes’ tactical maxim of combat at sea is truer today than ever before—the panoceanic Navy must attack effectively first. It must do everything possible to complete its kill chain while delaying the enemy’s, because once the chain starts it is very difficult to stop. Winning that race requires excellent scouting, robust C2 systems, effective counterscouting and, above all else, offensive weapons of maximum number, range, and destructiveness.55
However, given the realities of modern combat at sea and the large number of cross-domain forces, the Navy will not always win the scouting race, will not always be able to attack first, and will not always be able to defend against the enemy’s attacks. So, it must be prepared to suffer heavy losses. It must distribute its weapons and capabilities to as many platforms as possible to reduce the fleetwide impact of individual platform losses. Modern sensors, networks, and weapons enable such a fleet; platforms can be small and geographically distributed while still able to launch powerful individual attacks or coordinate to concentrate their firepower. Distribution allows for platforms of smaller signatures that can better leverage the basic physical opacity of water and earth to hide. It is no coincidence that submarines and land-based missiles remain among the most difficult to locate and thus among the most survivable forces in modern
warfare.56 The panoceanic Navy has both the need and the opportunity to distribute its capabilities to unprecedented degrees.
Public Support
Huntington showed that the second key element to any military service is the resources necessary to implement its strategic concept. He wrote, “The resources which a service is able to obtain in a democratic society are a function of the public support of that service. The service has the responsibility to develop this necessary support, and it can only do this if it possesses a strategic concept which clearly formulates its relationship to the national security.”57
For the transition from the Oceanic Phase to the Transoceanic Phase at the end of World War II, because there were no threats at sea, Huntington wrote that the Navy needed to “recognize that it is no longer the premier service but is one of three equal services all of which are essential to the implementation of American Cold War policy.”58 That balance between services will not be true for the panoceanic Navy. Given the geography of the Indo-Pacific—a combatant command named for not one but two oceans—the Navy is best-positioned of all the services to deter and, if necessary, defeat the PLA and defend the homeland. If the Navy can reorient itself away from the mission of the transoceanic Navy to instead focus on sea control and sea denial against the PLA and other competitors, it will have a strategic concept that can convince a skeptical public, represented by elected leaders, that it can best support national objectives that are integrally and directly connected to the safety and prosperity of the American people, and thus is deserving of an increased portion of the nation’s limited resources.
The Air Force will be a key player in such a fight but cannot lead it because of basing challenges. It must either operate large numbers of short-range fighters from Pacific bases without the concealment, range from the threat, or defenses to remain operational, or operate small numbers of long-range bombers from bases so far from the scene of action as to limit the mass and tempo at which they can deliver weapons.59
The Army was the logical service to lead land-centric conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan, but it is far from the logical choice to lead a maritime-centric conflict against China. It is difficult to envision a scenario in which hundreds of thousands of soldiers cross the Pacific Ocean to engage the PLA, and Ukraine has singlehandedly stalled the advance of the world’s other primary ground threat, weakening the Army’s case for resources in both theaters. Finally, the Marine Corps has already recognized the new threat and shifting national objectives and adjusted its strategic concept with Force Design, earning the service the resources to implement it. However, its strategic concept is largely in support of, not in competition with, the Navy, as the Marine Corps seeks to enable sea-control and sea-denial efforts.60
Given the scope and scale of the threat, the Navy cannot establish sea control and sea denial without the support of the rest of the joint force and the nation’s allies and partners. However, it is the only service that can lead such a fight. If the Navy can adjust its strategic concept, it has a clear opportunity to earn the required resources and use those resources to defend the nation against its greatest threat.
If one believes the change in threat and national objectives have made it appropriate to rebalance the allocation of resources among the services, then it also is appropriate to rebalance the allocation of resources among the Navy’s warfare communities. This already has started: the submarine force has a clear and convincing strategic concept within the panoceanic Navy, and it has earned resources to increase its size.61 The problem is that further increases in resources for building submarines likely will have diminishing returns given production limitations, with a similar problem affecting aircraft carrier construction.
As the British First Lord of the Admiralty said in 1903, the Royal Navy’s admirals “must cease to say ‘This is the ideal plan; how can we get enough money to carry it out?’ They must say instead ‘Here is a sovereign; how much can we squeeze out of it that will really count for victory in a Naval War?”62 While the nation makes the industrial-base investments necessary to increase submarine shipbuilding capacity in the medium-to-long term, the two areas that can “squeeze out” the best value from increased resources are the surface fleet and RASs.
For the transoceanic Navy, the primary weapon was the aerial bomb because of its precision and low cost. Because only aircraft could deliver aerial bombs, the surface fleet instead focused on protecting and enabling those aircraft and aircraft carriers—a mission at which it excelled. It conducted the same defensive mission for the transoceanic Navy’s other primary weapon, amphibious power. For the panoceanic Navy, however, missiles are the best weapon, and instead of being limited to aircraft as with aerial bombs, they can be launched by every platform in the fleet. It is a fundamental shift allowing the surface fleet to reorient itself from a defensive to an offensive force, an opportunity it identified a decade ago.63
As is true for any fleet, the panoceanic Navy needs to maximize the number, range, and destructiveness of its offensive weapons. To do so, aircraft and submarines are both excellent options, but also expensive ones. Furthermore, they both impose limits on weapon number and size: Aircraft are limited by weight and submarines by space. In contrast, surface warships can move heavy objects over long distance with fewer weight and size restrictions—and thus reduced cost—simply because they are buoyant. Buoyancy is the reason approximately 80 percent of the world’s trade moves by sea and why it is roughly 50 times more expensive to move heavy objects by air than by sea.64 Aircraft carriers and submarines must be large, either to launch aircraft or to achieve economies of scale to warrant the expense of nuclear power and undersea technologies. Missile-armed surface ships, however, do not need to be large and can be tailored to the weapon, instead of the other way around, allowing for the construction of large or small warships carrying large or small numbers of large or small weapons. The surface fleet introduced the Navy’s DMO concept, and it has the best opportunity to turn it into reality. Its admirals, senior officers, young tacticians, and supporting analysts have begun to push more determinedly for the changes necessary to do so.65
The most important change—both to turn the surface fleet into an offensive force and to make DMO a reality—is the introduction of new ship-based offensive weapons. When introducing the DMO concept (then called distributed lethality), the three admirals serving as the surface fleet’s type commanders and resource sponsor identified multiple capabilities that would better enable that force. All four of their top priorities were new weapons: an offensive surface-to-surface weapon; a low-cost medium-range strike weapon; a long-range ASW weapon; and a railgun. Yet none of those four have reached the fleet a decade later.66 Warships are simply platforms for weapons, sensors, and systems and the people who employ them. The panoceanic Navy is in desperate need of better offensive weapons.67
Robotic and autonomous systems are also poised to make great contributions to the Navy’s new mission. RASs can be platforms for weapons (such as missile-armed MUSVs), platforms for sensors (such as MQ-4 Tritons), or weapons themselves (such as one-way attack USVs). As platforms, they can be built with smaller sizes and signatures and can be operated with more risk than comparable manned platforms, enabling them to deliver weapons into highly contested areas. As weapons, RASs can be tailored to the mission and geography and built less expensively than high-end missiles, giving operational commanders more firepower and more options to use it.
Today, RASs are often added to the fleet to support manned platforms, such as using MUSVs as adjunct batteries for destroyers protecting carriers or using UASs as refuelers for manned aircraft. However, RASs tend to operate in fundamentally different ways than manned platforms, so forcing them to operate together limits RASs’ size, firepower, endurance, and utility, while also imposing operational restrictions on the host platform. Eventually, just as the Navy of the 1940s freed submarines from fleet escort and scout duties to operate independently and offensively with their own tactics, it must free RASs from defending or enabling manned platforms to yield fundamental improvements in their potential. Just as with the birth of naval aviation and the introduction of submarines, it will take experimentation, doctrine development, and time to develop RASs to their full potential.68
If surface fleet and RAS force leaders can capitalize on their advantages to develop credible strategic concepts, they can earn the resources to implement those concepts. They also can broaden their base of public support by expanding the shipbuilding industrial base to states not historically involved with warship construction.
Today, most naval shipbuilding is concentrated in Connecticut, Rhode Island, Maine, Virginia, Mississippi, California, and Wisconsin. Aircraft carrier and submarine production is even more limited because of those ships’ size, complexity, and nuclear power plants. In contrast, the surface fleet and RAS forces have the option to build smaller platforms, which can be built in more shipyards and factories across the country. For example, states such as Florida, Texas, Washington, South Carolina, Louisiana, New York, and New Jersey have dozens of small commercial shipyards, none that can build aircraft carriers or submarines but many that may be able to build MUSVs or corvettes.69 Smaller RASs further expand that potential industrial base.70
Huntington wrote that as a service’s strategic role changes, it may be necessary “for the service to expand, contract, or alter its sources of public support.” That will certainly be true for the panoceanic Navy, and its need for smaller distributed forces can expand its shipbuilding base while developing new sources of public support.
Naval Organization
The third and final element of a military service, according to Huntington, is its organizational structure, which allows it to best manage its resources to implement its strategic concept. As a service’s strategic concept changes, Huntington wrote, it may be necessary “to revamp its organizational structure in light of this changing mission.”
For the panoceanic Navy, most of the service’s necessary organization already exists. The exception is that it is necessary to maximize RAS warfighting potential. To date, RASs primarily have been organized by the domain in which they operate: naval aviation manages unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), the surface fleet manages unmanned surface vessels (USVs), and the submarine force manages unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs). There are benefits to this, but it also siloes learning and may not be the best way to organize those resources. RASs often cross domains, and UUVs, USVs, and UAVs may have more in common with each other than with strike fighters, guided-missile destroyers, or nuclear-powered submarines, respectively. Attempting to achieve a unity of effort while building, training, maintaining, or operating RASs isolated in domain-specific organizations can lead to blurred authorities, responsibilities, and resourcing, creating bureaucratic friction and delays. Finally, the people in the air, surface, and submarine communities responsible for developing RASs will inevitably be pulled in two directions, as described by Captain Jeffrey Kline: “a professional desire to create a system of systems to address specific naval missions, and the daily reality of supporting their organizations’ primary mission of manned component support.”71
Pioneers of naval aviation recognized aircraft as a fundamentally new tool of war and so sought to reorganize the Navy to capitalize on that potential. By building organizations such as the Bureau of Aeronautics, they created the structure to develop, protect, and advance naval aviation. Similarly, Admiral Hyman Rickover realized the fundamental changes and opportunities of nuclear power and so built a new organization—Naval Reactors—to capitalize on that technology’s potential. As another fundamentally new tool of war, RASs require their own organizational changes and support to reach their potential. The Department of the Navy already has recognized this and taken recent steps to address it, including a new deputy assistant secretary for RASs, a program executive office, and a portfolio acquisition executive.72 It will likely require additional organizational changes within the Navy itself—including a dedicated resource sponsor, type commander, and operational commanders—to best advocate for, advance, and operate RASs.73
Naval aviation pioneers also recognized the need for their own community and career path. For an innovation to flourish, it must attract talented people willing to devote great time and energy to achieve the necessary levels of expertise and experience, and that means the career path must be attractive. Early aviators fought for their own community, allowing them to recruit top-notch officers and sailors, develop them through a dedicated career instead of occasional aviation tours, and produce senior leaders able to advocate for their community and people. Whereas the interwar Royal Navy largely treated aviators as second-class officers compared with those serving on ships, with detrimental effects on the service, the U.S. Navy made the career path attractive and naval aviation flourished as a result.74
To develop the expertise, experience, and advocacy required for RASs, the people supporting them likely will require their own community. Despite the best intentions, leadership of unmanned forces is often perceived to be inferior to command of a fighter squadron, destroyer, or submarine, making it difficult to attract or retain the talent necessary to develop RASs to their full potential. Currently, a career focused on RASs is unlikely to lead to O-5 command, never mind flag rank, limiting the number of senior officers likely to advocate for such forces. The Navy has taken steps to address this, including introducing the robotics warfare enlisted rating and a surface warfare unmanned career path.75 To best attract talent, develop experience and expertise, and produce advocates, a separate RAS officer community may be necessary.
Finally, the service must consider how it can best organize its forces to implement this strategic concept. With the rise of the PLA in the 2010s, the service responded by basing approximately 60 percent of the fleet in the Pacific. The threat has grown since then, meaning an even greater portion of the fleet is needed. There will continue to be a demand for U.S. naval forces in other regions, as the competition with the PLA is now global and there are other threats to consider. However, if the Navy prevails in secondary theaters, it must still prevail in the Pacific to achieve its strategic aims; if the Navy prevails in the Pacific, then victory is almost assured in secondary theaters.
One could argue that the service should not let the China threat distract from its global responsibilities. In fact, the service should not let secondary responsibilities distract it from its primary responsibility of conducting its primary mission against its primary adversary in its primary theater.
Into the Future
For eight decades the logic of Huntington’s transoceanic Navy defined the Navy’s strategic concept. The service embraced that concept, earned the resources necessary to implement it, and repeatedly supported national objectives by commanding the seas and projecting power ashore. It is a proud era in U.S. naval history.
Today, the principal threat, national policy, and underlying assumptions have all changed; so too must the Navy’s strategic concept. As Huntington wrote:
"Shifts in the international balance of power will inevitably bring about changes in the principal threats to the security of any given nation. These must be met by shifts in national policy and corresponding changes in service strategic concepts. A military service capable of meeting one threat to the national security loses its reason for existence when that threat weakens or disappears. If the service is to continue to exist, it must develop a new strategic concept."76
Emerging from this period of great transition is the theory of the panoceanic Navy. The Navy’s ability to develop and improve that strategic concept and articulate it to the American people will determine its ability to win resources, reorganize itself for this era, and defend the nation against its greatest threat.
